plantings.variety: Super Star + Yellow Sweet Spanish (~18 each)

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Inspect for hornworms — apply Bt if found
tasks.description: Check undersides of leaves on lower third of plant. Look for dark frass (droppings) as first sign. If hornworms found: spray Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) on affected foliage per label. Hand-pick any large worms already present. Reapply Bt after rain.

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Inspect for early blight
tasks.description: Look for brown spots with yellow halos on lower leaves. Remove affected leaves immediately and dispose (do not compost). Increase airflow by pruning dense interior foliage if severe.

tasks.title: Pre-fruit feed — bone meal
tasks.description: Side-dress 1 tbsp bone meal per plant once first flowers appear. Supports fruit set and meets phosphorus demand during flowering.

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Inspect for hornworms — apply Bt if found
tasks.description: Check undersides of leaves on lower third of plant. Look for dark frass (droppings) as first sign. If hornworms found: spray Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) on affected foliage per label. Hand-pick any large worms already present. Reapply Bt after rain.

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Inspect for early blight
tasks.description: Look for brown spots with yellow halos on lower leaves. Remove affected leaves immediately and dispose (do not compost). Increase airflow by pruning dense interior foliage if severe.

tasks.title: Inspect for hornworms — apply Bt if found
tasks.description: Check undersides of leaves on lower third of plant. Look for dark frass (droppings) as first sign. If hornworms found: spray Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) on affected foliage per label. Hand-pick any large worms already present. Reapply Bt after rain.

tasks.title: Mid-season feed — blood meal
tasks.description: Work 1 tbsp blood meal per plant into top inch of soil. Apply once heavy fruiting begins to compensate for nitrogen demand. Skip if foliage is already very dark green — excess nitrogen pushes leaf growth at expense of fruit.

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Inspect for hornworms — apply Bt if found
tasks.description: Check undersides of leaves on lower third of plant. Look for dark frass (droppings) as first sign. If hornworms found: spray Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) on affected foliage per label. Hand-pick any large worms already present. Reapply Bt after rain.

tasks.title: Mid-season feed — fish emulsion
tasks.description: Half-strength fish emulsion (1 tbsp per gallon) once in early June to support continuous fruiting through summer. Do not overfeed — excess nitrogen pushes foliage at the expense of berry production.

tasks.title: Inspect for early blight
tasks.description: Look for brown spots with yellow halos on lower leaves. Remove affected leaves immediately and dispose (do not compost). Increase airflow by pruning dense interior foliage if severe.

tasks.title: First harvest check
tasks.description: Roma and paste types: firm and fully colored. Slicers: slight give when squeezed. Do not wait for perfect color in heat — fully vine-ripened tomatoes go soft fast in summer temperatures.

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Inspect for hornworms — apply Bt if found
tasks.description: Check undersides of leaves on lower third of plant. Look for dark frass (droppings) as first sign. If hornworms found: spray Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) on affected foliage per label. Hand-pick any large worms already present. Reapply Bt after rain.

tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Inspect for early blight
tasks.description: Look for brown spots with yellow halos on lower leaves. Remove affected leaves immediately and dispose (do not compost). Increase airflow by pruning dense interior foliage if severe.

tasks.title: Inspect for hornworms — apply Bt if found
tasks.description: Check undersides of leaves on lower third of plant. Look for dark frass (droppings) as first sign. If hornworms found: spray Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) on affected foliage per label. Hand-pick any large worms already present. Reapply Bt after rain.

tasks.title: Stop watering
tasks.description: Stop all irrigation 2 weeks before expected harvest. Dry conditions cure the outer wrapper and improve storage life significantly. Italian Red (softneck, W4) may be ready a week or two before Elephant & Music (hardneck, W3).

tasks.title: Stop watering
tasks.description: Stop all irrigation 2 weeks before expected harvest. Dry conditions cure the outer wrapper and improve storage life significantly. Italian Red (softneck, W4) may be ready a week or two before Elephant & Music (hardneck, W3).

tasks.title: Harvest check
tasks.description: Harvest when lower 3-4 leaves have browned but upper 5-6 are still green. Do not wait until all leaves are brown — the wrapper will deteriorate. Dig carefully with a fork, do not pull by the stem.

tasks.title: Harvest check
tasks.description: Harvest when lower 3-4 leaves have browned but upper 5-6 are still green. Do not wait until all leaves are brown — the wrapper will deteriorate. Dig carefully with a fork, do not pull by the stem.

tasks.title: Fall renovation
tasks.description: After last harvest in fall, cut all foliage back to 1 inch above crown. Remove old mulch and debris. Add 1 inch of fresh compost to the bed. Apply fall Berry-Tone feeding at label rate. This resets the plant for next year.

tasks.title: Early spring feed — Berry-Tone
tasks.description: Apply True Organic Berry-Tone at label rate (approximately 1/2 cup per 5 sq ft of bed) as soon as new growth appears in early spring. Work lightly into top inch of soil and water in well.

payload.headline: Sun through Saturday — tackle overdue tomato checks now
weather_notes: Zero precipitation through at least Saturday — no washoff risk, making this the ideal window to apply Bt if hornworms are found on e1–e4.
Highs of 74–77°F Thursday through Saturday are the cleanest multi-day outdoor work stretch since transplant week.
Overnight lows of 45–49°F — no frost risk for any active planting through the weekend.
Beds last watered May 12; three-plus sunny days will draw down soil moisture — check tomato and bean beds by Friday.

surfaced_tasks.title: Inspect for hornworms — apply Bt if found
surfaced_tasks.reason: Three days overdue and the zero-rain forecast through Saturday keeps any Bt applied now fully active without washoff risk.

surfaced_tasks.title: Inspect for early blight
surfaced_tasks.reason: Eight days overdue — the first check on the e1–e4 transplants has been skipped; catch anything early before warm dry conditions accelerate spread.

suggested_tasks.title: Resow missing spots in bean bed
suggested_tasks.description: Only 6 of 8 beans sprouted as of May 13; fill the 2 gaps now before 2-inch seedlings shade out anything sown later.

payload.headline: 94°F hits day-4 tomatoes — water now
weather_notes: 94°F today with zero precipitation — e1–e4 tomatoes are 4 days post-transplant and last irrigated May 9; wilt failure is likely without a deep soak today.
Wednesday hits 91°F with only a 20% storm chance — don't skip irrigation waiting for rain that probably won't come.
Wednesday night drops to 52°F and Thursday clears to 74°F — the heat breaks Thursday, the week's best outdoor work window.
Peas (c2–c4) logged at peak flower May 9 — two days of 90°F+ will cause significant blossom drop, shrinking the June 3 harvest.

suggested_tasks.title: Water e1–e4 tomatoes thoroughly today
suggested_tasks.description: Day-4 transplants last irrigated May 9; 94°F today will deplete soil moisture fast — a deep soak now prevents wilt and root damage.

payload.headline: 92-94°F heat dome hits day-2 tomato transplants
weather_notes: Today 92°F and Tuesday 94°F with zero rain — day-2 tomato transplants face maximum stress with no natural moisture relief through Wednesday.
No precipitation through Tuesday (0%) — all beds depend entirely on irrigation; zone 1 and 2 were last watered May 9.
Peas (c2–c4) at peak flower — sustained 90°F+ temps trigger blossom drop and will reduce pod set for the June 3 harvest.
Wednesday brings a slight T-storm chance (14%) — if rain arrives, hold next irrigation and check soil moisture before watering again.

surfaced_tasks.title: Deep-water e1–e4 tomatoes before Monday heat
surfaced_tasks.reason: Overdue by one day — today IS the 92°F event and day-2 transplants need a deep soak now before Tuesday hits 94°F.

suggested_tasks.title: Mulch base of e1–e4 tomato transplants
suggested_tasks.description: A 2-3 inch mulch layer on day-2 transplants cuts soil temperature and halves moisture loss during back-to-back 90°F+ days.

payload.headline: 92°F Monday hits day-2 tomato transplants hard
weather_notes: Monday 92°F and Tuesday 95°F with zero rain — the hottest stretch yet, arriving when e1–e4 tomatoes are only 24–48 hours in the ground.
No precipitation through at least Tuesday (0% all days) — irrigation is the only moisture source for every bed this week.
Overnight lows climb to 53–63°F Monday–Tuesday nights, eliminating overnight heat relief and keeping fresh transplants stressed around the clock.
Peas (c2–c4) logged at peak flower May 9 — temperatures above 85°F trigger blossom drop, and Tuesday's 95°F will cut set yield.

suggested_tasks.title: Deep-water e1–e4 tomatoes before Monday heat
suggested_tasks.description: Transplants are day 2 — a deep soak today before 92°F Monday is the single best protection against transplant shock and wilting failure.

payload.headline: 93°F Monday — water young beds by Sunday
weather_notes: Monday reaches 93°F — first heat spike of the season — with zero rain forecast all week.
Today (76°F) and Sunday (82°F) are the last two cooler windows before the heat arrives.
No precipitation through at least Monday; zone 2 was last watered May 7.
Overnight lows of 48–54°F pose no frost risk, but warm nights will accelerate surface moisture loss.

suggested_tasks.title: Water c1, w1, w4 before Monday heat spike
suggested_tasks.description: Beans are mid-germination (day 6) and 93°F Monday with no rain could stall or kill germination — water today or Sunday.

payload.headline: 84°F Sunday — harvest Sugar Bon before pods toughen
weather_notes: Sunday reaches 84°F with zero rain forecast all week — newly sown beds (w1, w4, c1) will dry fast; water by Saturday.
No precipitation through the week (0–2% daily) — clean window for any outdoor task; no washoff risk.
Overnight lows of 48–55°F — no frost risk for any active planting, no covers needed.
Fri–Sun all sunny and clear — best three-day outdoor work stretch of the season so far.

suggested_tasks.title: Harvest Sugar Bon snap peas now
suggested_tasks.description: Day 61 from planting against a 55-day DTM — pods are overdue and Sunday's 84°F will accelerate toughening and splitting.

payload.headline: Sunny and dry all week; Sugar Bon peas overdue
weather_notes: No rain through Saturday (0–4% daily) — no natural irrigation coming; any pest or foliar treatment applied this week will hold.
Highs of 77–79°F Thursday through Saturday offer a clean, extended outdoor work window.
Overnight lows of 44–52°F — no frost risk for any active planting; no covers needed.
Warm dry conditions will continue to dry raised beds — last watering was May 3.

suggested_tasks.title: Harvest Sugar Bon snap peas now
suggested_tasks.description: Day 60 from planting — Sugar Bon matures at 55 days, pods are past peak and will toughen quickly in this week's 78°F+ heat.

payload.headline: Overnight rain clears; 80°F heat arrives Friday
weather_notes: NWS Special Weather Statement active as of 2:17 AM today — check NWS SLC before outdoor work.
Tonight's 65-70% rain provides natural irrigation; skip Wednesday watering for all beds.
Wednesday clears to sunny 62°F — the week's cleanest window for outdoor tasks.
Thursday-Friday warm to 77-80°F, first real heat of the season; newly sown beds will dry out fast.

suggested_tasks.title: Check c1 bean moisture before Thursday heat
suggested_tasks.description: Beans sown May 3 need consistent soil moisture through germination; overnight rain covers Wednesday, but 77-80°F heat arriving Thursday will dry the bed before the 7-10 day germination window closes.

payload.headline: Storms today; dry Wednesday opens the week
weather_notes: Today's 65% storm chance delivers natural irrigation — skip manual watering through Wednesday.
Wednesday clears to sunny and dry (62°F, 5% precip) — the best outdoor work window this week.
Thursday warms to 76°F with 0% precip — a second clean outdoor day if needed.
Tonight's low of 42°F poses no frost risk to any active planting.

suggested_tasks.title: Check onion emergence, resow if needed
suggested_tasks.description: Onions sown April 25 hit day 14 on May 9 — if no sprouts visible by then, resow before soil warms past the ideal germination window.

payload.headline: Clear today, storms Monday — water new beds now
weather_notes: Today is dry at 78°F (2% precip) — the only reliable outdoor window before Monday's 50% afternoon storm risk.
Monday–Tuesday carry 50–52% precip chances; if storms miss, newly sown w1 and w4 won't get enough natural moisture to skip irrigation.
If Monday–Tuesday rain does land, hold irrigation and check bed moisture Wednesday before watering again.
Tuesday night low 42°F — no frost risk for any active planting this week.

suggested_tasks.title: Water strawberry and onion beds before storms
suggested_tasks.description: Two days since last irrigation and 78°F today — water w1 and w4 now since Monday's 50% storm chance isn't guaranteed to deliver enough moisture for newly sown beds.

payload.headline: Clear today, storms Monday — check snap peas now
weather_notes: Today 78°F and sunny (2% precip) is the only dry window before Monday's 50% T-storm chance.
Monday–Tuesday both carry ~50% precip — natural rainfall will cover irrigation for all beds mid-week.
Post-storm wet conditions Wednesday raise slug pressure on newly established w1 and w4.
Tuesday night drops to 42°F — no frost risk for any active planting.

suggested_tasks.title: Check Sugar Bon snap peas for harvest
suggested_tasks.description: Sugar Bon matures in 55 days — at day 56, pods are ready now and mature pods split after rain.

payload.headline: Inspect peas today — Monday storms could wash out Bt
weather_notes: Today 74°F and sunny (0% rain) is the cleanest window this week to inspect peas and apply Bt before storm risk arrives.
Monday brings 44% chance of showers and thunderstorms — Bt applied today or Sunday gets two full dry days to activate.
Overnight lows hold 42–53°F through the week — no frost risk for newly sown strawberries (w1) or onions (w4).
If Monday rain arrives, hold irrigation on w1 and w4 — newly sown beds won't need water mid-week.

surfaced_tasks.title: Inspect for aphids — apply Bt if caterpillars present
surfaced_tasks.reason: Due May 7 and today's dry window is the last clean Bt application opportunity before Monday's 44% storm risk.

surfaced_tasks.title: Inspect for aphids — apply Bt if caterpillars present
surfaced_tasks.reason: Due May 7 and today's dry window is the last clean Bt application opportunity before Monday's 44% storm risk.

surfaced_tasks.title: Inspect for aphids — apply Bt if caterpillars present
surfaced_tasks.reason: Due May 7 and today's dry window is the last clean Bt application opportunity before Monday's 44% storm risk.

payload.headline: Pea harvest due tomorrow — sunny and dry all week
weather_notes: No rain through Sunday (<10% daily chance) — any pest treatment this week will stick without washoff risk.
Saturday 75°F and Sunday 77°F are the best outdoor windows; first pea harvest checks are due tomorrow.
Overnight lows hold 40–51°F all week — no frost risk for any current planting.
Sunday night brings the first real rain chance (21%) — wrap outdoor tasks before then.

surfaced_tasks.title: Inspect for slugs
surfaced_tasks.reason: Due today — newly planted strawberries coming off last week's rain events face peak slug pressure as beds begin to dry out.

surfaced_tasks.title: First harvest check
surfaced_tasks.reason: Due tomorrow and at day 54 from planting pods are sizing up — Saturday's 75°F sunny window is ideal for picking at peak sweetness (also check c3/c4, tasks 17 and 5, same due date).

suggested_tasks.title: Water new strawberry and onion beds
suggested_tasks.description: Six days without rain or irrigation and 75°F+ temps this weekend put newly planted w1 and w4 at establishment-moisture risk.

payload.headline: Scapes 4 days overdue; pea checks due Saturday
weather_notes: Afternoon storm risk today (20% after noon) — finish any outdoor tasks before midday.
Friday is the cleanest window: 67°F and sunny with only 5% precip chance.
Saturday reaches 75°F and sunny — ideal conditions right as pea first-harvest checks come due (May 2).
No frost risk through the weekend; overnight lows hold 39–49°F.

surfaced_tasks.title: Check hardneck garlic for scapes
surfaced_tasks.reason: Four days overdue — use Friday's clear window to snap any scapes before continued curl diverts more energy from bulb development.

surfaced_tasks.title: First harvest check
surfaced_tasks.reason: Due Saturday and the 75°F sunny forecast is the best window of the week to check pods across all three pea beds (c2, c3, c4).
